[F:K]=\dim_K F.

若 [F:K]<\infty，称 F/K 为有限扩张。

例

  1. \mathbb C/\mathbb R 是次数为 2 的扩张：

    [\mathbb C:\mathbb R]=2.

  2. \mathbb R/\mathbb Q 不是有限扩张：

    [\mathbb R:\mathbb Q]=\infty.

备注

域扩张中的“次数”类似群论中子群指数的概念，但要注意：选取不同基底只改变表示方式，不改变维数。

entry.title: Abstract Algebra 作业
entry.content:
Abstract Algebra 作业

HW7

题目

Recall in our course, all rings have identity elements.

  1. A ring R such that a^2=a for all a\in R is called a Boolean ring. Prove that every Boolean ring R is commutative and a+a=0 for all a\in R.

  2. Prove: if A is the abelian group \mathbb Z\oplus \mathbb Z, then \operatorname{End} A is a non-commutative ring.

  3. A finite ring with more than one element and no zero divisors is a division ring.

  4. An element of a ring is nilpotent if a^n=0 for some n. Prove that in a commutative ring a+b is nilpotent if a and b are nilpotent. Show that this result may be false if R is not commutative. (Give counterexamples in the non-commutative case.)

  5. In a ring R the following conditions are equivalent.

    (a) R has no nonzero nilpotent elements.

    (b) If a\in R and a^2=0, then a=0.

答案

  1. Since a^2=a, applying this to a+a gives

    a+a=(a+a)^2=a^2+a^2+a^2+a^2=4a.

    Hence 2a=0 for every a\in R.

    Applying the Boolean condition to a+b,

    a+b=(a+b)^2=a^2+ab+ba+b^2=a+ab+ba+b.

    Thus ab+ba=0. Since every element has additive inverse equal to itself, this means ab=ba. Therefore R is commutative and a+a=0 for all a\in R.

  2. Identify \operatorname{End}(\mathbb Z\oplus\mathbb Z) with 2 imes 2 integer matrices, where addition is matrix addition and multiplication is composition, i.e. matrix multiplication.

    Let

    A= egin{pmatrix}0&1\0&0\end{pmatrix},\qquadB= egin{pmatrix}0&0\1&0\end{pmatrix}.

    Then
    AB= egin{pmatrix}1&0\0&0\end{pmatrix},\qquadBA= egin{pmatrix}0&0\0&1\end{pmatrix}.

    Hence AB e BA, so \operatorname{End}A is non-commutative.

  3. Let R be finite, |R|>1, and suppose R has no zero divisors. For any nonzero a\in R, the map

    L_a:R o R,\qquad x\mapsto ax

    is injective, since ax=ay implies a(x-y)=0, hence x-y=0. Since R is finite, L_a is surjective. Therefore there exists b\in R such that ab=1.

    Similarly the map R_a:x\mapsto xa is injective and hence surjective, so there exists c\in R such that ca=1. Then

    c=c(ab)=(ca)b=b.

    Thus b is a two-sided inverse of a. Every nonzero element is invertible, so R is a division ring.

  4. Suppose R is commutative, a^m=0, and b^n=0. By the binomial theorem,

    (a+b)^{m+n-1}=\sum_{i=0}^{m+n-1} inom{m+n-1}{i}a^i b^{m+n-1-i}.

    For each term, either i\ge m or m+n-1-i\ge n. Hence every term is 0, so (a+b)^{m+n-1}=0. Therefore a+b is nilpotent.

    In the non-commutative ring M_2(F), take

    A= egin{pmatrix}0&1\0&0\end{pmatrix},\qquadB= egin{pmatrix}0&0\1&0\end{pmatrix}.

    Then A^2=B^2=0, so both are nilpotent. But
    A+B= egin{pmatrix}0&1\1&0\end{pmatrix},\qquad(A+B)^2=I.

    Thus A+B is not nilpotent.

  5. (a)\Rightarrow(b) is immediate: if a^2=0, then a is nilpotent, so a=0.

    For (b)\Rightarrow(a), suppose a is nilpotent and a e0. Let k be minimal such that a^k=0. Since a e0, we have k>1. Put m=\lceil k/2 ceil. Then m<k, so a^m e0, but

    (a^m)^2=a^{2m}=0.

    This contradicts (b). Hence there are no nonzero nilpotent elements.

HW8

题目

  1. Let I be an ideal in a commutative ring R. Let

    \operatorname{Rad} I=\{r\in R\mid r^n\in I ext{ for some }n\}.

    Show that \operatorname{Rad} I is also an ideal.

  2. Let F be a field. Let S=\operatorname{Mat}_{2 imes 2}(F) be the ring of 2 by 2 matrices over F. Recall the center of a ring consists of elements commuting with all elements. Prove the center of S consists of all matrices of the form

    egin{pmatrix}a&0\0&a\end{pmatrix}.

  3. Let F be a field. Let S=\operatorname{Mat}_{2 imes 2}(F) be the ring of 2 by 2 matrices over F. Show that the center of S is not an ideal in S.

  4. Let F be a field. Let S=\operatorname{Mat}_{n imes n}(F) be the ring of n imes n matrices over F. What is its center? Prove your answer.

  5. Let R be a ring and S the ring of all n imes n matrices over R. Prove that J is an ideal of S if and only if J is the set of all n imes n matrices over I for some ideal I in R.

答案

  1. Let a,b\in\operatorname{Rad}I. Then a^m\in I and b^n\in I for some m,n. Since R is commutative,

    (a-b)^{m+n-1}=\sum_i inom{m+n-1}{i}a^i(-b)^{m+n-1-i}.

    Each term has either i\ge m or m+n-1-i\ge n, so each term lies in I. Hence a-b\in\operatorname{Rad}I.

    If r\in R, then

    (ra)^m=r^m a^m\in I,

    so ra\in\operatorname{Rad}I. Therefore \operatorname{Rad}I is an ideal.

  2. Let

    A= egin{pmatrix}a&b\c&d\end{pmatrix}

    be central. Commuting with
    E_{11}= egin{pmatrix}1&0\0&0\end{pmatrix}

    gives
    AE_{11}= egin{pmatrix}a&0\c&0\end{pmatrix},\qquadE_{11}A= egin{pmatrix}a&b\0&0\end{pmatrix}.

    Thus b=c=0. So A= egin{pmatrix}a&0\0&d\end{pmatrix}.

    Now commute with

    E_{12}= egin{pmatrix}0&1\0&0\end{pmatrix}.

    Then
    AE_{12}= egin{pmatrix}0&a\0&0\end{pmatrix},\qquadE_{12}A= egin{pmatrix}0&d\0&0\end{pmatrix}.

    Hence a=d. Therefore the center is exactly {aI_2:a\in F}.

  3. The center contains I_2. If it were an ideal of S, then for every A\in S we would have AI_2=A in the center. That would force every matrix to be central, which is false. For example E_{12} is not central. Hence the center is not an ideal.

  4. The center of \operatorname{Mat}_{n imes n}(F) is

    Z(S)=\{aI_n:a\in F\}.

    Indeed, if A=(a_{ij}) commutes with every matrix unit E_{rs}, then AE{rs}=E{rs}A for all r,s. Comparing entries shows all off-diagonal entries of A are 0, and all diagonal entries are equal. Thus A=aI_n. Conversely, every scalar matrix aI_n commutes with all matrices.

  5. Suppose I is an ideal of R. Let \operatorname{Mat}_n(I) be the set of matrices whose entries lie in I. It is closed under addition and additive inverses. If A\in \operatorname{Mat}_n(I) and B\in \operatorname{Mat}_n(R), then each entry of AB and BA is a finite sum of elements of the form r i or i r, so lies in I. Hence \operatorname{Mat}_n(I) is an ideal of S.

    Conversely, let J be an ideal of S. Define

    I=\{r\in R:\ r ext{ is the }(1,1) ext{-entry of some matrix in }J\}.

    Then I is an ideal of R: addition and negatives are clear, and if r\in I occurs as the (1,1)-entry of A\in J, then s r and r s occur as (1,1)-entries of sE_{11}A and AE{11}sE{11}, which lie in J.

    Now take any A=(a_{ij})\in J. For each i,j,

    E_{1i}AE_{j1}

    has a_{ij} in the (1,1)-entry and 0 elsewhere, so a_{ij}\in I. Thus J\subseteq\operatorname{Mat}_n(I).

    Conversely, if r\in I, choose A\in J whose (1,1)-entry is r. Then

    E_{i1}AE_{1j}

    is the matrix with r in the (i,j)-entry and 0 elsewhere, so every matrix unit multiple rE_{ij} lies in J. Hence every matrix over I lies in J. Therefore J=\operatorname{Mat}_n(I).

HW9

题目

All rings in this HW are commutative rings with identity.

  1. Let R be a commutative ring with identity and suppose that the ideal A of R is contained in a finite union of prime ideals

    P_1\cup\cdots\cup P_n.

    Show that A\subset P_i for some i.

  2. Let f:R o S be an epimorphism of rings with kernel K.

    (a) If P is a prime ideal in R that contains K, then f(P) is a prime ideal in S.

    (b) If Q is a prime ideal in S, then f^{-1}(Q) is a prime ideal in R that contains K.

    (c) There is a one-to-one correspondence between the set of all prime ideals in R that contain K and the set of all prime ideals in S, given by P\mapsto f(P).

    (d) If I is an ideal in a ring R, then every prime ideal in R/I is of the form P/I, where P is a prime ideal in R that contains I.

  3. An ideal M e R in a commutative ring R with identity is maximal if and only if for every r\in R-M, there exists x\in R such that 1_R-rx\in M.

  4. Consider the infinite direct product ring

    R=\prod_{i=1}^{\infty}\mathbb Z.

    Construct an ideal I such that I is not of the form
    \prod_{i=1}^{\infty} I_i,

    where each I_i is an ideal of \mathbb Z.

答案

  1. We prove by prime avoidance. Suppose no P_i contains A. Removing redundant primes if necessary, assume for each j that

    A\cap P_j ot\subseteq igcup_{i e j}P_i.

    Choose
    a_j\in (A\cap P_j)- igcup_{i e j}P_i.

    Then
    x=a_1+a_2a_3\cdots a_n

    lies in A. But x otin P_1, because a_1\in P_1 while a_2\cdots a_n otin P_1 by primality. For j\ge2, we have a_2\cdots a_n\in P_j but a_1 otin P_j, so x otin P_j. Hence x otin P_1\cup\cdots\cup P_n, contradicting A\subseteq igcup_iP_i. Therefore A\subseteq P_i for some i.

  2. (a) Since f is surjective, f(P) is an ideal of S. Also f(P) e S, because if 1_S\in f(P), then some p\in P has f(p)=1_S, so p-1_R\in K\subseteq P, hence 1_R\in P, impossible.

    If ab\in f(P), choose r,s\in R with f(r)=a, f(s)=b. Then f(rs)\in f(P), so rs-p\in K for some p\in P. Since K\subseteq P, we get rs\in P. Since P is prime, r\in P or s\in P, so a\in f(P) or b\in f(P). Thus f(P) is prime.

    (b) The preimage f^{-1}(Q) is an ideal and contains K=f^{-1}(0). It is proper because 1_S otin Q. If ab\in f^{-1}(Q), then f(a)f(b)=f(ab)\in Q. Since Q is prime, f(a)\in Q or f(b)\in Q, so a\in f^{-1}(Q) or b\in f^{-1}(Q). Thus f^{-1}(Q) is prime.

    (c) The two maps are inverse. If P\supseteq K, then

    f^{-1}(f(P))=P.

    Indeed, if x\in f^{-1}(f(P)), then f(x)=f(p) for some p\in P, so x-p\in K\subseteq P, hence x\in P. The reverse inclusion is clear. Also, because f is surjective,
    f(f^{-1}(Q))=Q

    for every ideal Q of S. Hence prime ideals of S correspond exactly to prime ideals of R containing K.

    (d) Apply (c) to the quotient map \pi:R o R/I. The kernel is I. Thus every prime ideal of R/I is \pi(P)=P/I for some prime ideal P\supseteq I.

  3. If M is maximal, then R/M is a field. For r otin M, the coset r+M is nonzero, so it has an inverse x+M. Hence

    (r+M)(x+M)=1+M,

    meaning 1-rx\in M.

    Conversely, suppose the stated condition holds. Let J be an ideal with M\subsetneq J\subseteq R. Choose r\in J-M. By assumption, there exists x\in R such that 1-rx\in M. Since r\in J, we have rx\in J, and since M\subseteq J, we get 1=(1-rx)+rx\in J. Thus J=R. Hence M is maximal.

  4. Let

    I= igoplus_{i=1}^{\infty}\mathbb Z

    be the set of integer sequences with finite support. This is an ideal of R=\prod_{i=1}^{\infty}\mathbb Z, because multiplying a finite-support sequence coordinatewise by any sequence still gives finite support.

    Suppose I=\prod_{i=1}^{\infty} I_i. For each coordinate i, the sequence with 1 in the i-th position and 0 elsewhere lies in I, so I_i=\mathbb Z for every i. Then \prod_i I_i=\prod_i\mathbb Z=R, but I e R, since (1,1,1,\ldots) otin I. Contradiction. Therefore I is not of product form.

HW10

题目

Feel free to use the following easy fact:

Let d\in\mathbb Z. Let

\mathbb Z[\sqrt d]=\{x+y\sqrt d\mid x,y\in\mathbb Z\}.
Then it is a subring of \mathbb C.1. Show \mathbb Z[\sqrt{-2}] is a Euclidean domain.2. Show \mathbb Z[\sqrt2] is a Euclidean domain.3. Consider R=\mathbb Z[\sqrt{-3}]. Consider elements
a=4=2\cdot2=(1+\sqrt{-3})(1-\sqrt{-3}),\qquad b=(1+\sqrt{-3})\cdot2.
Show that a,b have no greatest common divisor.4. Consider the ring A=\mathbb Z[i]. (a) The principal ideal (2) generated by 2 is not a prime ideal. (b) The principal ideal (3) of A is a prime ideal. (c) The principal ideal (5) of A is not a prime ideal. (d) The principal ideal (7) of A is a prime ideal.### 答案1. Use the norm
N(a+b\sqrt{-2})=a^2+2b^2.
For \alpha, eta\in\mathbb Z[\sqrt{-2}] with eta e0, write \alpha/ eta=x+y\sqrt{-2} with x,y\in\mathbb Q. Choose integers m,n such that |x-m|\le1/2 and |y-n|\le1/2. Let q=m+n\sqrt{-2} and r=\alpha-q eta. Then
rac{r}{ eta}=(x-m)+(y-n)\sqrt{-2}.
Thus
N(r)=N( eta) ig((x-m)^2+2(y-n)^2 ig).
A nearest-lattice-point argument for the lattice \mathbb Z[\sqrt{-2}] in \mathbb C gives a choice of m,n with
(x-m)^2+2(y-n)^2<1.
Hence N(r)<N( eta). Therefore \mathbb Z[\sqrt{-2}] is Euclidean.2. Use
N(a+b\sqrt2)=|a^2-2b^2|.
For \alpha, eta\in\mathbb Z[\sqrt2] with eta e0, write
\alpha/ eta=x+y\sqrt2
with x,y\in\mathbb Q. Choose integers m,n such that |x-m|\le1/2 and |y-n|\le1/2. Let q=m+n\sqrt2 and r=\alpha-q eta. Then
N(\alpha/ eta-q)=|(x-m)^2-2(y-n)^2|<1,
since (x-m)^2\le1/4 and 2(y-n)^2\le1/2. Hence
N(r)=N( eta)N(\alpha/ eta-q)<N( eta).
Thus division with smaller remainder exists, so \mathbb Z[\sqrt2] is Euclidean.3. Let s=\sqrt{-3}. The elements 2 and 1+s are both common divisors of
a=4,\qquad b=2(1+s).
Suppose a greatest common divisor d exists. Since both 2 and 1+s are common divisors, we must have
2\mid d,\qquad 1+s\mid d.
Also d\mid4 and d\mid2(1+s). Use the norm N(x+ys)=x^2+3y^2. Since d\mid4, N(d) divides N(4)=16. Since 2\mid d and 1+s\mid d, we have 4=N(2)\mid N(d) and 4=N(1+s)\mid N(d). Therefore N(d) is either 4 or 16. If N(d)=4, then d is associated to either 2 or 1+s. But 2 and 1+s are not associates, since (1+s)/2 otin\mathbb Z[s]. If N(d)=16, then because d\mid4, d is associated to 4. But 4 mid b=2(1+s), since b/4=(1+s)/2 otin\mathbb Z[s]. Both cases are impossible. Hence a,b have no greatest common divisor.4. In \mathbb Z[i], use the norm
N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2.

(a) We have

(1+i)(1-i)=2\in(2),

but 1+i otin(2) and 1-i otin(2). Hence (2) is not prime.

(b) Suppose (a+bi)(c+di)\in(3). Then 3 divides

N((a+bi)(c+di))=(a^2+b^2)(c^2+d^2).

Since 3 is prime in \mathbb Z, 3\mid a^2+b^2 or 3\mid c^2+d^2. Modulo 3, the only squares are 0 and 1, so a^2+b^2\equiv0\pmod3 implies a\equiv b\equiv0\pmod3. Hence a+bi\in(3); similarly for c+di. Therefore (3) is prime.

(c) Since

5=(2+i)(2-i),

we have (2+i)(2-i)\in(5), but neither 2+i nor 2-i is in (5). Hence (5) is not prime.

(d) Suppose (a+bi)(c+di)\in(7). Taking norms gives

7\mid (a^2+b^2)(c^2+d^2).

If 7\mid a^2+b^2, then modulo 7 the squares are 0,1,2,4. The congruence a^2+b^2\equiv0\pmod7 forces a\equiv b\equiv0\pmod7, since -1 is not a square modulo 7. Thus a+bi\in(7). Similarly for the other factor. Hence (7) is prime.

HW11

题目

  1. Let F be a field. Prove F[x] is a Euclidean domain.

  2. (a) If F is a field, then every nonzero element of Fx is of the form x^k u with u\in Fx a unit.

    (b) Fx is a principal ideal domain whose only ideals are 0, Fx=(1_F)=(x^0), and (x^k) for each k\ge1.

3.

(a) The polynomial x+1 is a unit in the power series ring \mathbb Zx, but is not a unit in \mathbb Z[x].

(b) x^2+3x+2 is irreducible in \mathbb Zx, but not in \mathbb Z[x].

  1. If c_0,c_1,\ldots,c_n are distinct elements of an integral domain D and d_0,\ldots,d_n are any elements of D, then there is at most one polynomial f of degree \le n in D[x] such that f(c_i)=d_i for i=0,1,\ldots,n.

答案

  1. Let f,g\in F[x] with g e0. The polynomial division algorithm over a field gives

    f=qg+r,\qquad r=0 ext{ or }\deg r<\deg g.

    Therefore F[x] is Euclidean with Euclidean function \delta(f)=\deg f for nonzero f.

  2. (a) Let

    f=a_kx^k+a_{k+1}x^{k+1}+\cdots

    where a_k e0 and k is minimal. Then
    f=x^k(a_k+a_{k+1}x+\cdots)=x^ku.

    Since the constant term of u is a_k e0, u is a unit in Fx.

    (b) Let I be a nonzero ideal of Fx. Choose f\in I with minimal order k, meaning f=x^ku where u is a unit. Then x^k=fu^{-1}\in I, so (x^k)\subseteq I. Conversely, every element of I has order at least k, so it is divisible by x^k. Hence I\subseteq(x^k). Therefore I=(x^k). The zero ideal is the remaining case.

  3. (a) In \mathbb Zx,

    (1+x)(1-x+x^2-x^3+\cdots)=1,

    so 1+x is a unit. In \mathbb Z[x], the only units are \pm1, so 1+x is not a unit.

    (b) In \mathbb Z[x],

    x^2+3x+2=(x+1)(x+2),

    so it is reducible.

    In \mathbb Zx, x+1 is a unit, so x^2+3x+2 is associated to x+2. If

    x+2=fg

    in \mathbb Zx, then the constant terms of f and g multiply to 2. Thus one constant term is \pm1, so one factor is a unit. Hence x+2 is irreducible, and therefore x^2+3x+2 is irreducible in \mathbb Zx.

  4. Suppose f,g\in D[x] both have degree \le n and satisfy f(c_i)=g(c_i)=d_i. Then h=f-g has degree \le n and has roots c_0,\ldots,c_n. Since D is an integral domain, a nonzero polynomial of degree \le n has at most n roots. But h has n+1 distinct roots, so h=0. Hence f=g.

HW12

题目

  1. Let R be a commutative ring with identity and c,b\in R with c a unit.

    (a) Show that the assignment x\mapsto cx+b induces a unique automorphism of R[x] that is the identity on R. What is its inverse?

    (b) If D is an integral domain, then show that every automorphism of D[x] that is the identity on D is of the type described in (a).

  2. Prove that x^5+48x+24 is irreducible in \mathbb Q[x].

  3. Let

    f=\sum_{i=0}^n a_ix^i\in\mathbb Z[x]

    have degree n. Suppose that for some k with 0<k<n and some prime p, we have:

    • p mid a_n and p mid a_k;
    • p\mid a_i for all 0\le i\le k-1;
    • p^2 mid a_0.

    Show that f has a factor g of degree at least k that is irreducible in \mathbb Z[x].

  4. Let H be a subgroup of G. The centralizer of H is

    C_G(H)=\{g\in G\mid hg=gh ext{ for all }h\in H\}.

    Show that C_G(H) is a normal subgroup of N_G(H).

  5. Use the setup in the above problem. Prove:

    N_G(H)/C_G(H)

    is isomorphic to a subgroup of \operatorname{Aut}H.

  6. Find all the Sylow 2-subgroups and 3-subgroups of S_5.

  7. If |G|=200, then G contains a normal Sylow subgroup.

答案

  1. (a) By the universal property of polynomial rings, any choice of an element of R[x] determines a unique R-algebra homomorphism R[x] o R[x]. Thus x\mapsto cx+b induces a unique homomorphism \varphi fixing R.

    Since c is a unit, define

    \psi(x)=c^{-1}x-c^{-1}b.

    Then
    \psi(\varphi(x))=\psi(cx+b)=c^{-1}(cx+b)-c^{-1}b=x,

    and
    \varphi(\psi(x))=\varphi(c^{-1}x-c^{-1}b)=c^{-1}(cx+b)-c^{-1}b=x.

    Both maps fix R, so \psi=\varphi^{-1}. Hence \varphi is an automorphism.

    (b) Let \varphi be an automorphism of D[x] fixing D. Put f=\varphi(x). Since \varphi is surjective, there exists g\in D[x] such that g(f)=x. Taking degrees over the integral domain D gives

    \deg(g(f))=(\deg g)(\deg f)=1.

    Hence \deg f=1. Therefore
    f=cx+b

    with c e0. Applying the same argument to \varphi^{-1} shows that c must be a unit. Hence every such automorphism has the form in (a).

  2. The polynomial

    f(x)=x^5+48x+24

    is Eisenstein at p=3: the leading coefficient is not divisible by 3, every other coefficient is divisible by 3, and the constant term 24 is not divisible by 9. Hence f is irreducible in \mathbb Z[x], and by Gauss’s lemma it is irreducible in \mathbb Q[x].

  3. Factor f into irreducibles in \mathbb Z[x]:

    f=g_1g_2\cdots g_m.

    Reducing modulo p, the hypotheses say
    \overline f=x^k\overline h

    in \mathbb F_p[x], where \overline h(0)=\overline{a_k} e0 and the leading coefficient is nonzero.

    Since p\mid a_0 but p^2 mid a_0, exactly one irreducible factor g_j has constant term divisible by p. All other factors have nonzero constant term modulo p. Therefore all of the factor x^k in \overline f must come from \overline{g_j}. Hence x^k\mid\overline{g_j}, so

    \deg g_j\ge k.

    This g_j is irreducible in \mathbb Z[x] and has degree at least k.

  4. First C_G(H)\le N_G(H), because if c commutes with every element of H, then cHc^{-1}=H.

    Let n\in N_G(H) and c\in C_G(H). For any h\in H, since n^{-1}hn\in H, we have

    c(n^{-1}hn)=(n^{-1}hn)c.

    Multiplying by n on the left and n^{-1} on the right gives
    (ncn^{-1})h=h(ncn^{-1}).

    Thus ncn^{-1}\in C_G(H). Therefore C_G(H) rianglelefteq N_G(H).

  5. Define

    \Phi:N_G(H) o\operatorname{Aut}(H)

    by
    \Phi(n)(h)=nhn^{-1}.

    Since n\in N_G(H), conjugation by n maps H to itself, so \Phi(n) is an automorphism of H. Also \Phi is a group homomorphism.

    Its kernel consists of those n\in N_G(H) such that nhn^{-1}=h for every h\in H, i.e.

    \ker\Phi=C_G(H).

    By the first isomorphism theorem,
    N_G(H)/C_G(H)\cong \operatorname{Im}\Phi,

    and \operatorname{Im}\Phi is a subgroup of \operatorname{Aut}H.

  6. Since

    |S_5|=120=2^3\cdot3\cdot5,

    a Sylow 2-subgroup has order 8, and a Sylow 3-subgroup has order 3.

    The Sylow 3-subgroups are exactly

    \langle(abc) angle

    where {a,b,c} runs over all 3-element subsets of {1,2,3,4,5}. There are inom53=10 of them.

    The Sylow 2-subgroups are obtained as follows. Choose one element a to be fixed, and partition the remaining four elements into two unordered pairs:

    \{b,c\}\sqcup\{d,e\}.

    Then
    P_{a;\{b,c\},\{d,e\}}=\langle (bc),\ (de),\ (bd)(ce) angle

    is a subgroup of order 8, isomorphic to D_8. These are all Sylow 2-subgroups. There are
    5\cdot3=15

    such subgroups.

  7. Since

    |G|=200=2^3\cdot5^2,

    the number n_5 of Sylow 5-subgroups satisfies
    n_5\equiv1\pmod5,\qquad n_5\mid 8.

    The divisors of 8 are 1,2,4,8, and the only one congruent to 1 modulo 5 is 1. Hence n_5=1. Therefore the Sylow 5-subgroup is unique, and hence normal.

entry.title: Une matinée à la bibliothèque
entry.content:
Une matinée à la bibliothèque

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/lectures-francaises/2026-06-01-matinee-bibliotheque/

Niveau

A1-A2. Texte original court pour lecture quotidienne.

Texte

Ce matin, Lina arrive à la bibliothèque de son quartier à neuf heures. Elle porte un sac léger avec un cahier, un stylo bleu et une petite bouteille d’eau. À l’entrée, elle dit bonjour à la bibliothécaire et rend un roman facile. Ensuite, elle cherche une table près de la fenêtre, parce que la lumière est claire et douce. Sur la table, elle pose son téléphone en mode silencieux. Elle veut lire trente minutes sans regarder les messages. D’abord, elle choisit un livre pour enfants avec des phrases courtes. Elle lit une page lentement, puis elle souligne trois mots nouveaux. Dans son cahier, elle écrit le mot, la traduction et une phrase personnelle. Quand elle ne comprend pas, elle regarde le dessin ou elle relit la phrase. Elle écoute aussi les petits bruits de la salle: des pages tournent, une chaise bouge, quelqu’un chuchote. Cette ambiance calme l’aide à rester attentive. À dix heures, un ami arrive et s’assoit en face d’elle. Ils parlent doucement de leur cours de français. Avant de partir, Lina emprunte le livre et note la date de retour. Elle sort contente, car elle a appris peu de mots, mais elle les connaît mieux.

Traduction phrase par phrase

Français中文
Ce matin, Lina arrive à la bibliothèque de son quartier à neuf heures.今天早上，Lina 九点到达她社区的图书馆。
Elle porte un sac léger avec un cahier, un stylo bleu et une petite bouteille d’eau.她背着一个轻便的包，里面有一本笔记本、一支蓝色钢笔和一小瓶水。
À l’entrée, elle dit bonjour à la bibliothécaire et rend un roman facile.在入口处，她向图书管理员问好，并归还一本简单的小说。
Ensuite, elle cherche une table près de la fenêtre, parce que la lumière est claire et douce.然后，她找一张靠窗的桌子，因为光线明亮而柔和。
Sur la table, elle pose son téléphone en mode silencieux.她把手机放在桌上，并调成静音模式。
Elle veut lire trente minutes sans regarder les messages.她想读三十分钟，不看消息。
D’abord, elle choisit un livre pour enfants avec des phrases courtes.首先，她选择一本儿童书，里面有短句。
Elle lit une page lentement, puis elle souligne trois mots nouveaux.她慢慢读一页，然后划出三个新词。
Dans son cahier, elle écrit le mot, la traduction et une phrase personnelle.在笔记本里，她写下单词、翻译和一个自己的句子。
Quand elle ne comprend pas, elle regarde le dessin ou elle relit la phrase.当她不理解时，她看插图，或者重读句子。
Elle écoute aussi les petits bruits de la salle: des pages tournent, une chaise bouge, quelqu’un chuchote.她也听着阅览室里的细小声音：书页翻动、椅子移动、有人低声说话。
Cette ambiance calme l’aide à rester attentive.这种安静的氛围帮助她保持专注。
À dix heures, un ami arrive et s’assoit en face d’elle.十点，一个朋友来了，坐在她对面。
Ils parlent doucement de leur cours de français.他们轻声谈论他们的法语课。
Avant de partir, Lina emprunte le livre et note la date de retour.离开前，Lina 借走这本书，并记下归还日期。
Elle sort contente, car elle a appris peu de mots, mais elle les connaît mieux.她开心地走出去，因为她学的新词不多，但掌握得更好了。

Vocabulaire

Mot / Expression中文Note
bibliothèque图书馆aller à la bibliothèque = 去图书馆
quartier街区；社区son quartier = 她的社区
rendre归还rendre un livre = 还一本书
près de在……附近près de la fenêtre = 靠近窗户
silencieux安静的；静音的mode silencieux = 静音模式
souligner划线；强调souligner un mot = 划出一个词
emprunter借入emprunter un livre = 借一本书
date de retour归还日期常用于图书馆场景

Grammaire

PointExplication
à + lieu表示地点或方向，如 à la bibliothèque、à l’entrée。
vouloir + infinitifElle veut lire = 她想读。vouloir 后接动词原形。
sans + infinitifsans regarder = 不看；表示“不做某事”。
parce que / car都可说明原因，parce que 常用于句中，car 更书面。
quand表示“当……的时候”，后面接一个完整句子。

Phrases utiles

Phrase中文
Elle cherche une table près de la fenêtre.她找一张靠窗的桌子。
Elle veut lire sans regarder les messages.她想读书，不看消息。
Elle note la date de retour.她记下归还日期。
Elle a appris peu de mots, mais elle les connaît mieux.她学的新词不多，但掌握得更好了。

entry.title: Le carnet de quartier
entry.content:
Le carnet de quartier

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/lectures-francaises/2026-06-05-carnet-de-quartier/

Niveau

A1-A2. Texte original court pour lecture quotidienne.

Texte

Chaque vendredi, Lina marche dans son quartier avec un petit carnet bleu. Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle. Ce matin, elle sort après le petit-déjeuner et ferme doucement la porte. Dans la rue des Pins, une boulangère met des croissants dans la vitrine. Lina écrit: “La boulangerie ouvre à sept heures.” Plus loin, deux enfants jouent devant l’école, et un homme arrose les fleurs de son balcon. Lina écoute les sons: un vélo, un bus, puis le rire d’une voisine. Elle dessine aussi un banc sous un grand arbre, parce qu’elle aime lire à cet endroit. À midi, elle achète une soupe simple au café du coin. Le serveur lui demande si elle habite ici depuis longtemps. Lina répond: “Non, seulement depuis un mois, mais je me sens déjà bien.” L’après-midi, elle rentre chez elle et relit ses notes. Elle ajoute trois mots nouveaux: calme, vitrine et voisinage. Le soir, elle téléphone à sa soeur et décrit son quartier en français. Grâce à ce carnet, Lina remarque les petits détails et parle avec plus de confiance.

Traduction phrase par phrase

Français中文
Chaque vendredi, Lina marche dans son quartier avec un petit carnet bleu.每个星期五，Lina 带着一本蓝色小笔记本在她的街区散步。
Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle.她想更好地了解家附近的街道。
Ce matin, elle sort après le petit-déjeuner et ferme doucement la porte.今天早上，她吃完早餐后出门，并轻轻关上门。
Dans la rue des Pins, une boulangère met des croissants dans la vitrine.在松树街，一位女面包师把羊角面包放进橱窗。
Lina écrit: “La boulangerie ouvre à sept heures.”Lina 写道：“面包店七点开门。”
Plus loin, deux enfants jouent devant l’école, et un homme arrose les fleurs de son balcon.再往前，两个孩子在学校前玩耍，一个男人给阳台上的花浇水。
Lina écoute les sons: un vélo, un bus, puis le rire d’une voisine.Lina 听着这些声音：一辆自行车、一辆公交车，然后是一位女邻居的笑声。
Elle dessine aussi un banc sous un grand arbre, parce qu’elle aime lire à cet endroit.她还画了一张大树下的长椅，因为她喜欢在这个地方读书。
À midi, elle achète une soupe simple au café du coin.中午，她在街角咖啡馆买了一份简单的汤。
Le serveur lui demande si elle habite ici depuis longtemps.服务员问她是否已经在这里住了很久。
Lina répond: “Non, seulement depuis un mois, mais je me sens déjà bien.”Lina 回答：“没有，只住了一个月，但我已经感觉很舒服了。”
L’après-midi, elle rentre chez elle et relit ses notes.下午，她回到家，重新读自己的笔记。
Elle ajoute trois mots nouveaux: calme, vitrine et voisinage.她添加了三个新词：安静、橱窗和邻里。
Le soir, elle téléphone à sa soeur et décrit son quartier en français.晚上，她给姐姐打电话，并用法语描述自己的街区。
Grâce à ce carnet, Lina remarque les petits détails et parle avec plus de confiance.多亏了这本笔记本，Lina 注意到小细节，说话也更有信心。

Vocabulaire

Mot / Expression中文Note
quartier街区；社区dans mon quartier = 在我的街区
carnet笔记本un petit carnet bleu
près de在……附近près de chez elle = 在她家附近
vitrine橱窗la vitrine d’un magasin
arroser浇水arroser les fleurs
banc长椅un banc sous un arbre
café du coin街角咖啡馆du coin = 附近的、本地的
depuis自从；已经depuis un mois = 已经一个月
calme安静的；平静une rue calme
confiance信心parler avec confiance

Grammaire

PointExplication
Chaque + nom singulier表示“每一个”，后面接单数名词：chaque vendredi。
près de chez + pronom表示“在某人家附近”：près de chez elle。
depuis + durée表示一个状态持续了多久：depuis un mois。
grâce à + nom表示“多亏；由于”：grâce à ce carnet。

Phrases utiles

Phrase中文
Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle.她想更好地了解家附近的街道。
Je me sens déjà bien.我已经感觉很舒服了。
Elle décrit son quartier en français.她用法语描述自己的街区。
Elle parle avec plus de confiance.她说话更有信心。

entry.title: Darwin on Earthworms and Soil
entry.content:
Darwin on Earthworms and Soil

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/readings/2026-06-01-darwin-earthworms-soil/

Source

Charles Darwin, The Formation of Vegetable Mould, through the Action of Worms, with Observations on Their Habits (1881), public domain. Passage adapted from Darwin’s introduction and early observations.

Passage

Few animals appear less important at first sight than the earthworm. It has no beauty that attracts the eye, no strength that seems useful to human labor, and no sudden motion by which it can command attention. Yet Darwin argues that the quiet work of worms has changed the surface of the land more steadily than many forces that look more impressive. Their power lies not in violence but in repetition. A single worm carries only a small amount of fine earth to the surface; millions of worms, working through many seasons, can cover stones, bury old pavements, and prepare the soil in which plants take root.

The process begins with feeding. Worms swallow earth together with decaying leaves and other vegetable matter. Inside their bodies, the material is broken down and mixed. The castings that they leave at the mouth of their burrows are therefore not useless dirt but soil that has been sifted, enriched, and moved upward. Rain then spreads these castings over the ground. In this way, the upper layer of mould is continually renewed, while objects that once rested on the surface slowly sink from view.

Their burrows also change the condition of the ground. Channels made by worms allow air and water to enter soil that would otherwise become compact. Leaves pulled underground decay out of sight and add organic material close to plant roots. The animal that seems merely to disturb a garden path is therefore helping to make the earth more open, mixed, and fertile.

This gradual burial explains a fact that might otherwise seem puzzling. Old fields sometimes hide coins, tiles, or fragments of buildings beneath a layer of earth, although no human hand has intentionally covered them. The explanation is not that the objects actively descend, but that the soil rises around and above them. Worms bring up fine particles from below; weather distributes the particles; vegetation holds the new layer in place. What appears to be still ground is, in fact, a surface under slow construction.

Darwin’s point is also methodological. He asks readers to notice a small cause and then to enlarge their view of time. An effect too slight to impress us in a day may become decisive when multiplied by centuries. The earthworm therefore becomes evidence for a broader scientific habit: nature often works through ordinary agents whose importance can be measured only by patient observation.

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
at first sightwhen first noticedOften introduces a contrast with deeper analysis.
repetitionrepeated actionKey idea in passages about gradual natural processes.
castingssoil left by worms after digestionA technical term explained by context.
renewedmade fresh or restoredCommon in environmental science passages.
methodologicalrelated to a method of studySignals how evidence is being used, not just what happened.
decisivehaving a major final effectUseful for cause-and-effect questions.

Sentence Work

Their power lies not in violence but in repetition.

The structure is not in A but in B, which forces a contrast. The sentence tells us that the author’s focus is not dramatic natural force, but repeated small actions. In TOEFL reading, this kind of sentence often states the central explanation of a paragraph.

The explanation is not that the objects actively descend, but that the soil rises around and above them.

This sentence corrects a possible misunderstanding. The buried objects do not move downward by themselves; rather, worm activity and weather gradually build soil upward. The phrase not that... but that... is a useful pattern for replacing a wrong cause with the correct one.

Structure Notes

The passage moves from surprising contrast to process explanation. First it presents the earthworm as an unimpressive animal with a large cumulative effect. Then it explains the mechanism: feeding, digestion, castings, burrows, rain, and renewed soil. After that, it applies the mechanism to buried objects in old fields. The final paragraph widens the lesson from earthworms to scientific thinking about small causes over long periods.

Writing Takeaway

To explain a gradual process, begin with an ordinary object, state why it matters, break the process into visible steps, and end by showing the larger principle behind the example.

entry.title: Gilbert White on Swallows and Observation
entry.content:
Gilbert White on Swallows and Observation

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/readings/2026-06-05-gilbert-white-swallows-observation/

Source

Gilbert White, The Natural History and Antiquities of Selborne (1789), public domain. Passage adapted from White’s letters on swallows, migration, and local observation.

Passage

In Gilbert White’s account of Selborne, the arrival of swallows is not a decorative sign of spring but a problem that requires careful evidence. The birds appear suddenly in April, skim over meadows and ponds through the warm months, and then vanish when autumn advances. A careless observer might explain this pattern with a simple rule: the birds come when the weather is mild and leave when it grows cold. White treats that rule as only a starting point. He wants to know which species arrive first, where they feed, how they build, and whether every bird truly departs.

His method depends on repeated attention to a familiar place. Instead of collecting a single dramatic incident, he compares many small observations: the height at which swallows fly before rain, the insects they follow, the dates on which nests are occupied, and the behavior of young birds near the end of summer. Such details matter because a broad theory of migration can be tested only by facts that seem ordinary at first. If one swallow is seen late in the year, the sighting may be an accident; if several are recorded in the same conditions, the pattern becomes harder to ignore.

White also shows why scientific caution is valuable. In his time, some people believed that swallows slept through winter hidden in banks, hollow trees, or even beneath water. Others argued that the birds migrated to warmer regions. White does not dismiss either claim merely because it sounds strange or familiar. He asks what each explanation would predict and what local evidence can confirm. The question is not settled by imagination, but by the patient comparison of season, weather, species, and behavior.

The result is a model of natural history as disciplined noticing. Selborne is small, yet it becomes a field laboratory because the observer returns to the same lanes, roofs, streams, and hedges year after year. The value of the place lies in continuity. When observations accumulate, they reveal changes that no single walk could prove: the timing of arrivals, the relation between insects and flight, and the difference between rumor and evidence. White’s swallows therefore teach a larger lesson. Knowledge often begins when a common event is treated not as background scenery, but as a question that deserves exact description.

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
decorative signsomething noticed mainly for beauty or symbolismThe phrase is contrasted with evidence-based inquiry.
starting pointan initial idea, not a final answerUseful when an author refines a simple explanation.
repeated attentioncareful observation over timeA key method in natural history and field science.
dramatic incidenta striking single eventOften contrasted with accumulated data.
predictsay what should happen if an explanation is correctImportant in passages about testing hypotheses.
continuityregular connection over timeExplains why repeated observation has scientific value.

Sentence Work

Such details matter because a broad theory of migration can be tested only by facts that seem ordinary at first.

The main structure is cause and condition: details matter because.... The sentence explains why small observations are not trivial. The phrase can be tested only by shows necessity; without ordinary facts, the larger theory remains unproved.

White does not dismiss either claim merely because it sounds strange or familiar.

This sentence highlights scientific neutrality. The pattern not... merely because... warns readers that an idea should not be accepted or rejected by appearance alone. Evidence, not habit or surprise, controls the argument.

Structure Notes

The passage begins with a familiar seasonal event and turns it into a research question. It then explains White’s method of repeated local observation, contrasts single incidents with accumulated evidence, and places the debate over swallows within a larger lesson about cautious scientific reasoning. The final paragraph broadens the example from one village to the general value of disciplined noticing.

Writing Takeaway

When writing about observation, choose a common phenomenon, show why a simple explanation is incomplete, list the kinds of evidence needed, and end by stating the larger habit of thought the example teaches.

entry.title: John Burroughs on Birds and Patient Attention
entry.content:
John Burroughs on Birds and Patient Attention

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/readings/2026-06-06-burroughs-birds-attention/

Source

John Burroughs, Wake-Robin (1871), public domain. Passage adapted from Burroughs’s essays on birds, attention, and field observation.

Passage

In John Burroughs’s essays, the study of birds begins with a discipline that looks deceptively simple: standing still long enough for the woods to resume its ordinary life. A person who enters a field with noise and impatience may see only sudden motion, as if the whole place were made of accidents. The more practiced observer understands that the first confusion belongs less to the birds than to the visitor. Leaves move, calls overlap, and wings pass through the branches before the eye has learned which signs matter.

Burroughs treats attention as an acquired skill rather than a natural gift. The beginner wants the bird to appear fully identified, like a specimen in a case, but the living bird is rarely so convenient. It is known by fragments: a short song repeated from a hidden perch, a quick rise from grass, the color of a wing when light strikes it, or the particular way a body balances on a twig. Each sign may be insufficient alone. Together, however, they allow the observer to move from guess to recognition.

This method also changes the observer’s relation to place. A hedge or orchard that once seemed empty becomes crowded with evidence when visited at different hours and seasons. The same tree may serve as shelter in rain, a feeding station in the morning, and a lookout post at dusk. Migration adds another layer of meaning, because familiar species disappear while others arrive for only a brief interval. Because the evidence is scattered, the record must include absences as carefully as appearances. A silent thicket at noon may teach less than the same thicket at dawn, and a bird missing for a week may be as significant as one newly heard. The observer therefore learns to treat time itself as part of the specimen, not merely as the background against which the specimen is seen. To notice such changes, the observer must compare present sight with remembered pattern.

The lesson is not merely about naming birds. Burroughs suggests that nature rewards a form of patience that modern habits easily interrupt. Quick looking produces a catalogue of impressions; sustained looking reveals behavior, sequence, and relation. The bird is no longer a spot of color but a participant in a local system of weather, food, shelter, and season. Knowledge grows when attention slows down enough to let ordinary facts arrange themselves into an explanation.

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
deceptively simpleseeming easy while actually requiring careOften signals that the author will complicate an ordinary idea.
resumebegin again after a pauseHere it describes a place returning to normal after disturbance.
acquired skillan ability learned through practiceUseful in passages about observation, science, and education.
insufficientnot enough by itselfCommon in arguments that require combined evidence.
brief intervala short period of timeOften appears in discussions of seasons, migration, or experiments.
sustained lookingcareful attention maintained over timeContrasts with quick, superficial observation.

Sentence Work

The more practiced observer understands that the first confusion belongs less to the birds than to the visitor.

The main clause is the observer understands. The that clause explains what is understood: confusion is caused more by the visitor’s untrained attention than by the birds themselves. The phrase less to... than to... creates a comparison.

Quick looking produces a catalogue of impressions; sustained looking reveals behavior, sequence, and relation.

The semicolon links two parallel clauses. The contrast is not between seeing and not seeing, but between two qualities of seeing: quick attention gathers scattered details, while sustained attention explains how those details connect.

Structure Notes

The passage moves from a general discipline to a method of evidence. First it shows why stillness is necessary. Then it explains how birds are identified through partial signs rather than a complete view. The third paragraph extends observation across place, time, and season. The final paragraph states the broader lesson: patient attention turns ordinary details into explanation.

Writing Takeaway

To write about observation, begin with a simple action, show why it is harder than it looks, list the kinds of evidence a careful observer uses, and end by naming the larger habit of thought behind the method.

entry.title: Huxley on Reading a Piece of Chalk
entry.content:
Huxley on Reading a Piece of Chalk

Blog permalink: https://kalax524.com/readings/2026-05-28-huxley-piece-of-chalk/

Source

Thomas Henry Huxley, “On a Piece of Chalk,” in Discourses: Biological and Geological Essays (public domain). Text available via Project Gutenberg.

Passage

A great chapter of the history of the world is written in the chalk. Few passages in the history of man can be supported by such an overwhelming mass of direct and indirect evidence as that which testifies to the truth of the fragment of the history of the globe, which I hope to enable you to read, with your own eyes, to-night. Let me add, that few chapters of human history have a more profound significance for ourselves. I weigh my words well when I assert, that the man who should know the true history of the bit of chalk which every carpenter carries about in his breeches-pocket, though ignorant of all other history, is likely, if he will think his knowledge out to its ultimate results, to have a truer, and therefore a better, conception of this wonderful universe, and of man’s relation to it, than the most learned student who is deep-read in the records of humanity and ignorant of those of Nature.

The language of the chalk is not hard to learn, not nearly so hard as Latin, if you only want to get at the broad features of the story it has to tell; and I propose that we now set to work to spell that story out together.

We all know that if we “burn” chalk the result is quicklime. Chalk, in fact, is a compound of carbonic acid gas, and lime, and when you make it very hot the carbonic acid flies away and the lime is left. By this method of procedure we see the lime, but we do not see the carbonic acid. If, on the other hand, you were to powder a little chalk and drop it into a good deal of strong vinegar, there would be a great bubbling and fizzing, and, finally, a clear liquid, in which no sign of chalk would appear. Here you see the carbonic acid in the bubbles; the lime, dissolved in the vinegar, vanishes from sight. There are a great many other ways of showing that chalk is essentially nothing but carbonic acid and quicklime. Chemists enunciate the result of all the experiments which prove this, by stating that chalk is almost wholly composed of “carbonate of lime.”

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
overwhelming massa very large and persuasive amountOften describes evidence or data.
testifies togives evidence forAcademic synonym for shows or supports.
profound significancedeep importanceCommon in humanities and science passages.
ultimate resultsfinal consequences after full reasoningSignals that the author wants deep inference.
conceptiona formed understanding or ideaIn TOEFL, often means a theory-like view, not pregnancy.
broad featuresmain outlines, not tiny detailsUseful phrase for summarizing structure.
carbonic acid gascarbon dioxide, in older terminologyOlder scientific prose may use historical names.
enunciatestate clearly and formallyA high-value academic verb.
carbonate of limecalcium carbonateThe chemical substance that makes up chalk.

Sentence Work

Few passages in the history of man can be supported by such an overwhelming mass of direct and indirect evidence as that which testifies to the truth of the fragment of the history of the globe…

The main comparison is Few passages ... can be supported by such evidence as that.... Huxley contrasts human history with natural history: human records may be uncertain, but geological evidence is both direct and indirect. In TOEFL reading, a sentence like this often supports an author’s purpose question: he is raising the status of scientific evidence before explaining it.

The language of the chalk is not hard to learn, not nearly so hard as Latin, if you only want to get at the broad features of the story it has to tell.

The metaphor language of the chalk turns geology into reading. The phrase not nearly so hard as Latin lowers the reader’s fear, while broad features limits the promise: Huxley is not teaching every technical detail, only the main story that evidence can reveal.

Structure Notes

Huxley begins with a bold claim: a common piece of chalk contains a chapter of world history. Then he justifies why this ordinary object matters, arguing that natural evidence can give a deeper view of the universe than human records alone. After that, he shifts from philosophy to demonstration. Burning chalk and dissolving it in vinegar become simple experiments that reveal its chemical composition. The paragraph therefore moves from significance, to accessibility, to empirical proof.

Writing Takeaway

This passage is a useful model for explanatory writing. Start with a surprising claim, connect it to a larger question, reassure the reader that the idea can be understood, and then give a concrete demonstration. That order makes difficult science feel both important and approachable.

entry.title: Un message après le cours
entry.content:
Niveau

A1-A2. Texte original court pour lecture quotidienne.

Texte

Après le cours de français, Mei reste quelques minutes dans la salle. Les autres étudiants sortent vite, mais elle range lentement son livre et son cahier. Elle veut poser une question au professeur, pourtant le professeur parle déjà avec une autre étudiante. Mei ne veut pas interrompre la conversation. Elle prend donc son téléphone et écrit un message simple. D’abord, elle dit bonjour et remercie le professeur pour le cours. Ensuite, elle explique sa difficulté: elle ne comprend pas bien la différence entre “à” et “de” dans deux phrases du manuel. Elle ajoute une phrase polie: “Pouvez-vous donner un exemple, s’il vous plaît ?” Avant d’envoyer le message, Mei le relit deux fois. Elle corrige un accent, change un verbe et vérifie la formule de politesse. Le soir, le professeur répond avec trois exemples courts. Mei copie les exemples dans son cahier et écrit une phrase personnelle avec chacun. Le lendemain matin, elle relit ses notes pendant le petit déjeuner. Elle comprend qu’une bonne question peut devenir une petite leçon. Maintenant, elle a moins peur d’écrire en français.

Traduction phrase par phrase

Français中文
Après le cours de français, Mei reste quelques minutes dans la salle.法语课后，Mei 在教室里多待了几分钟。
Les autres étudiants sortent vite, mais elle range lentement son livre et son cahier.其他学生很快离开，但她慢慢收好书和笔记本。
Elle veut poser une question au professeur, pourtant le professeur parle déjà avec une autre étudiante.她想问老师一个问题，可是老师已经在和另一名女学生说话。
Mei ne veut pas interrompre la conversation.Mei 不想打断谈话。
Elle prend donc son téléphone et écrit un message simple.于是她拿出手机，写了一条简单的信息。
D’abord, elle dit bonjour et remercie le professeur pour le cours.首先，她问好，并感谢老师的课程。
Ensuite, elle explique sa difficulté: elle ne comprend pas bien la différence entre “à” et “de” dans deux phrases du manuel.然后，她说明自己的困难：她不太明白课本中两个句子里 “à” 和 “de” 的区别。
Elle ajoute une phrase polie: “Pouvez-vous donner un exemple, s’il vous plaît ?”她加上一句礼貌的话：“您可以举一个例子吗？”
Avant d’envoyer le message, Mei le relit deux fois.发送信息之前，Mei 把它重读了两遍。
Elle corrige un accent, change un verbe et vérifie la formule de politesse.她改正了一个重音符号，换了一个动词，并检查礼貌表达。
Le soir, le professeur répond avec trois exemples courts.晚上，老师用三个简短例子回复了她。
Mei copie les exemples dans son cahier et écrit une phrase personnelle avec chacun.Mei 把例子抄到笔记本里，并用每个例子写一个自己的句子。
Le lendemain matin, elle relit ses notes pendant le petit déjeuner.第二天早上，她在早餐时重读笔记。
Elle comprend qu’une bonne question peut devenir une petite leçon.她明白，一个好问题可以变成一堂小课。
Maintenant, elle a moins peur d’écrire en français.现在，她不那么害怕用法语写作了。

Vocabulaire

Mot / Expression中文Note
après le cours课后après + nom 表示“在……之后”。
ranger收拾；整理ranger son livre = 收好自己的书。
poser une question提问比 dire une question 更自然。
interrompre打断interrompre une conversation = 打断谈话。
difficulté困难expliquer sa difficulté = 说明自己的困难。
manuel课本；手册un manuel de français = 一本法语课本。
formule de politesse礼貌表达常用于邮件或短信结尾。
exemple例子donner un exemple = 举例。
chacun每一个avec chacun = 用每一个。
avoir moins peur de不那么害怕Elle a moins peur d’écrire.

Grammaire

PointExplication
vouloir + infinitifElle veut poser une question: veut 后接动词原形。
déjà表示“已经”：le professeur parle déjà.
ne … pas基本否定结构：elle ne comprend pas bien.
avant de + infinitif表示“在做某事之前”：avant d’envoyer le message.
pouvoir + infinitifPouvez-vous donner un exemple ? 用于礼貌请求。

Phrases utiles

Phrase中文
Je voudrais poser une question.我想问一个问题。
Pouvez-vous donner un exemple, s’il vous plaît ?您可以举一个例子吗？
Je ne comprends pas bien la différence.我不太明白这个区别。
Elle relit le message deux fois.她把信息重读了两遍。

entry.title: John Burroughs on Birds and Patient Attention
entry.content:
Source

John Burroughs, Wake-Robin (1871), public domain. Passage adapted from Burroughs’s essays on birds, attention, and field observation.

Passage

In John Burroughs’s essays, the study of birds begins with a discipline that looks deceptively simple: standing still long enough for the woods to resume its ordinary life. A person who enters a field with noise and impatience may see only sudden motion, as if the whole place were made of accidents. The more practiced observer understands that the first confusion belongs less to the birds than to the visitor. Leaves move, calls overlap, and wings pass through the branches before the eye has learned which signs matter.

Burroughs treats attention as an acquired skill rather than a natural gift. The beginner wants the bird to appear fully identified, like a specimen in a case, but the living bird is rarely so convenient. It is known by fragments: a short song repeated from a hidden perch, a quick rise from grass, the color of a wing when light strikes it, or the particular way a body balances on a twig. Each sign may be insufficient alone. Together, however, they allow the observer to move from guess to recognition.

This method also changes the observer’s relation to place. A hedge or orchard that once seemed empty becomes crowded with evidence when visited at different hours and seasons. The same tree may serve as shelter in rain, a feeding station in the morning, and a lookout post at dusk. Migration adds another layer of meaning, because familiar species disappear while others arrive for only a brief interval. Because the evidence is scattered, the record must include absences as carefully as appearances. A silent thicket at noon may teach less than the same thicket at dawn, and a bird missing for a week may be as significant as one newly heard. The observer therefore learns to treat time itself as part of the specimen, not merely as the background against which the specimen is seen. To notice such changes, the observer must compare present sight with remembered pattern.

The lesson is not merely about naming birds. Burroughs suggests that nature rewards a form of patience that modern habits easily interrupt. Quick looking produces a catalogue of impressions; sustained looking reveals behavior, sequence, and relation. The bird is no longer a spot of color but a participant in a local system of weather, food, shelter, and season. Knowledge grows when attention slows down enough to let ordinary facts arrange themselves into an explanation.

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
deceptively simpleseeming easy while actually requiring careOften signals that the author will complicate an ordinary idea.
resumebegin again after a pauseHere it describes a place returning to normal after disturbance.
acquired skillan ability learned through practiceUseful in passages about observation, science, and education.
insufficientnot enough by itselfCommon in arguments that require combined evidence.
brief intervala short period of timeOften appears in discussions of seasons, migration, or experiments.
sustained lookingcareful attention maintained over timeContrasts with quick, superficial observation.

Sentence Work

The more practiced observer understands that the first confusion belongs less to the birds than to the visitor.

The main clause is the observer understands. The that clause explains what is understood: confusion is caused more by the visitor’s untrained attention than by the birds themselves. The phrase less to... than to... creates a comparison.

Quick looking produces a catalogue of impressions; sustained looking reveals behavior, sequence, and relation.

The semicolon links two parallel clauses. The contrast is not between seeing and not seeing, but between two qualities of seeing: quick attention gathers scattered details, while sustained attention explains how those details connect.

Structure Notes

The passage moves from a general discipline to a method of evidence. First it shows why stillness is necessary. Then it explains how birds are identified through partial signs rather than a complete view. The third paragraph extends observation across place, time, and season. The final paragraph states the broader lesson: patient attention turns ordinary details into explanation.

Writing Takeaway

To write about observation, begin with a simple action, show why it is harder than it looks, list the kinds of evidence a careful observer uses, and end by naming the larger habit of thought behind the method.

entry.title: 抽代笔记整理
entry.content:
抽代笔记整理

说明：以下内容按原 PDF 的出现顺序整理，并把手写笔记中的定义、定理、例子与证明改写为较接近数学书的排版。明显划掉、重复或笔误的地方已按上下文修正。

1. 素理想与极大理想

定义：素理想

设 R 为交换环，P\subseteq R 为理想。若

  1. P e R；
  2. 对任意 x,y\in R，若 xy\in P，则 x\in P 或 y\in P，

则称 P 是 R 的一个素理想。

等价地，

P ext{ 是素理想}\iffR/P ext{ 是整环}.

例

  1. 在 R=\mathbb Z 中，(p) 是素理想当且仅当 p 是素数；(0) 也是素理想，因为 \mathbb Z 是整环。

  2. 在 R=\mathbb F_2[x] 中，(x^2+1) 不是素理想，因为

    x^2+1=(x+1)^2

在 \mathbb F_2[x] 中可约。

  1. 在 R=\mathbb C[x]/(x^2) 中，(0) 不是素理想，因为 [x]^2=0，但 [x] e 0。

定理：素理想的乘积判别

设 R 为交换环，P\subseteq R 为真理想。以下命题等价：

  1. P 是素理想；
  2. 对任意理想 A,B\subseteq R，若 AB\subseteq P，则 A\subseteq P 或 B\subseteq P。

证明。

若 P 是素理想，且 AB\subseteq P。若 A subseteq P 且 B subseteq P，取

a\in A\setminus P,\qquad b\in B\setminus P.

则 ab\in AB\subseteq P，与 P 为素理想矛盾。

反过来，若对理想乘积有上述性质，取 a,b\in R 且 ab\in P，则

(a)(b)\subseteq P.

于是 (a)\subseteq P 或 (b)\subseteq P，即 a\in P 或 b\in P。

定理：商环刻画素理想

设 R 为交换环，P\subseteq R 为真理想，则

P ext{ 是素理想}\iffR/P ext{ 是整环}.

证明。

在 R/P 中，

\overline{x}\,\overline{y}=0\iffxy\in P.

若 P 是素理想，则 xy\in P 推出 x\in P 或 y\in P，即 \overline{x}=0 或 \overline{y}=0，所以 R/P 无零因子。

反过来，若 R/P 是整环，则由 \overline{x}\,\overline{y}=0 得 \overline{x}=0 或 \overline{y}=0，即 x\in P 或 y\in P。

定义：偏序集与 Zorn 引理

设 A 为集合，关系 \leq 若满足：

  1. 自反性：a\leq a；
  2. 传递性：a\leq b,\ b\leq c\Rightarrow a\leq c；
  3. 反对称性：a\leq b,\ b\leq a\Rightarrow a=b，

则称 (A,\leq) 为一个偏序集。若 a\leq b 或 b\leq a，称 a,b 可比较。

若 B\subseteq A，元素 u\in A 满足对任意 b\in B 都有 b\leq u，则称 u 为 B 的上界。

Zorn 引理。 设 (A,\leq) 是非空偏序集。若 A 中每条链都有上界，则 A 含有极大元。

定理：极大理想存在

设 R 为有单位的交换环，且 R e 0。则 R 至少有一个极大理想。更强地，任意真理想都包含在某个极大理想中。

证明。

给定真理想 I\subsetneq R。令

\mathcal A=\{J\subseteq R:J ext{ 是理想且 } I\subseteq J\subsetneq R\}.

按包含关系排序。若 \mathcal C={C_i} 是一条链，令

M= igcup_i C_i.

则 M 是理想，并且 I\subseteq M。若 M=R，则 1\in M，于是 1\in C_i 对某个 i 成立，矛盾。因此 M\in\mathcal A。由 Zorn 引理，\mathcal A 有极大元，即包含 I 的极大理想。

定义：极大理想

设 R 为环，M\subseteq R 为理想。若

  1. M e R；
  2. 对任意理想 I，若 M\subseteq I\subseteq R，则 I=M 或 I=R，

则称 M 为极大理想。

定理：极大理想的商环刻画

设 R 为有单位交换环，M\subseteq R 为真理想。则

M ext{ 是极大理想}\iffR/M ext{ 是域}.

证明。

若 M 极大。取非零元 \overline a\in R/M，则 a otin M。由极大性，

M+(a)=R.

于是存在 m\in M,\ r\in R，使得

m+ra=1.

故 \overline r\,\overline a=\overline 1，所以 \overline a 可逆。

反过来，若 R/M 是域，设 M\subsetneq N\subseteq R 为理想。取 x\in N\setminus M，则 \overline x e 0。由于 R/M 是域，存在 \overline y 使

\overline x\,\overline y=\overline 1.

即 xy+m=1 对某个 m\in M 成立。因 xy\in N 且 m\in N，得 1\in N，故 N=R。

推论：极大理想是素理想

若 R 为交换环，M 是极大理想，则 M 是素理想。

理由。 R/M 是域，域一定是整环，因此 M 是素理想。

定理：域的等价刻画

设 R 为非零交换环。以下命题等价：

  1. R 是域；
  2. R 没有非平凡真理想；
  3. (0) 是极大理想；
  4. 任意非零环同态 f:R o S 都是单射。

说明。 若 R 是域，则任何非零理想含有非零元 a，而 a 可逆，所以 1=a^{-1}a 属于该理想。

2. 商环与理想对应

回忆：环同态与核

若 f:R o S 是环同态，则

R/\ker f \cong \operatorname{Im} f.

若 \ker f=R，则 f=0；若 \ker f={0}，则 f 单射。

理想对应定理

设 I\subseteq R 为理想。则有双射

\left\{ ext{$R/I$ 的理想} ight\}\longleftrightarrow\left\{ ext{$R$ 中包含 $I$ 的理想} ight\}.

具体地，

J/I\subseteq R/I\quad\leftrightarrow\quadJ\subseteq R,\ I\subseteq J.

特别地，若 I\subseteq J，则

(R/I)/(J/I)\cong R/J.

3. 有限直积与中国剩余定理

定理：环的直积

设 {Ri}{i\in I} 是一族环，则

\prod_{i\in I} R_i

在逐点加法和乘法下仍为环。

理想的直积

设 R=R_1 imes\cdots imes R_n。若 I_i\subseteq R_i 是理想，则

I_1 imes\cdots imes I_n

是 R 的理想。反过来，R 的任意理想 I 都可写成

I=I_1 imes\cdots imes I_n,

其中

I_i=\pi_i(I)\subseteq R_i.

证明关键是：若 x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in I，则通过乘以标准幂等元

e_i=(0,\dots,0,1,0,\dots,0)

可得 (0,\dots,x_i,\dots,0)\in I，从而 I 由各坐标部分生成。

例：\mathbb Z imes\mathbb Z 中的素理想

在 R=\mathbb Z imes\mathbb Z 中，理想形如 (a) imes(b)。商环为

R/ igl((a) imes(b) igr)\cong\mathbb Z/(a) imes\mathbb Z/(b).

该商环为整环时，只能其中一个因子为零环，另一个为整环。因此素理想形如

(p) imes \mathbb Z\quad ext{或}\quad\mathbb Z imes(p),

其中 p=0 或 p 为素数。

定理：中国剩余定理的存在唯一形式

设 A_1,\dots,A_n\subseteq R 为理想，且两两互素：

A_i+A_j=R,\qquad i e j.

则对任意 b_1,\dots,b_n\in R，存在 b\in R，使得

b\equiv b_i\pmod {A_i},\qquad i=1,\dots,n.

并且这样的 b 模

igcap_{i=1}^n A_i

唯一。

中国剩余定理的商环形式

若 A_1,\dots,A_n 两两互素，则自然同态

\varphi:R\longrightarrow \prod_{i=1}^n R/A_i,\qquadx\longmapsto (x+A_1,\dots,x+A_n)

诱导同构

R/ igcap_{i=1}^n A_i\cong\prod_{i=1}^n R/A_i.

例

求整数 b 使

b\equiv 2\pmod 5,\qquad b\equiv 3\pmod 6.

由于 (5,6)=1，解模 30 唯一。可取

b\equiv 27\pmod {30}.

4. 交换环中的因子分解

定义：整除与相伴

设 R 为交换环，a,b\in R。

  1. 若存在 x\in R 使 ax=b，则称 a 整除 b，记作 a\mid b。
  2. 若 a\mid b 且 b\mid a，则称 a,b 相伴，记作 a\sim b。

在有单位整环中，

a\sim b\iff(a)=(b)\iffa=ub ext{ for some }u\in R^ imes.

定义：不可约元、素元、可约元

设 R 为整环。

  1. 非零非单位元 c\in R 称为不可约元，若 c=ab 推出 a 或 b 是单位。

  2. 非零非单位元 p\in R 称为素元，若

    p\mid ab\Rightarrow p\mid a ext{ 或 }p\mid b.

  3. 非零非单位元 c 称为可约元，若 c=ab，其中 a,b 都不是单位。

例

  1. 在 \mathbb Z 中，不可约元等价于素元。
  2. 在 \mathbb Z/6\mathbb Z 中，\overline 2 是素元的直观判别会失效，因为环有零因子；因此素元概念通常放在整环中使用。

  3. 在

    R=\mathbb Z[\sqrt{-3}]=\{a+b\sqrt{-3}:a,b\in\mathbb Z\}

    中，1+\sqrt{-3} 可为不可约元，但不是素元。因为

    1+\sqrt{-3}\mid 4=(1+\sqrt{-3})(1-\sqrt{-3}),

    但它不整除 2。

定理：素元与素理想

设 R 为整环，x\in R 非零非单位。则

x ext{ 是素元}\iff(x) ext{ 是素理想}.

定理：不可约元与主理想的极大性

设 R 为整环，x\in R 非零非单位。若主理想 (x) 在所有真主理想中极大，则 x 不可约。

若 R 是 PID，则反过来也成立。因此在 PID 中，

x ext{ 不可约}\iff(x) ext{ 是真主理想中的极大元}.

进一步，在 PID 中不可约元都是素元。

定义：唯一分解整环

整环 R 称为唯一分解整环（UFD），若任意非零非单位元 x\in R 都可写成

x=p_1p_2\cdots p_n,

其中 p_i 为不可约元；并且若

x=q_1q_2\cdots q_m

也是不可约分解，则 m=n，且在重排后 p_i\sim q_i。

例

\mathbb Z,\quad \mathbb C[x],\quad \mathbb F[x]

都是 UFD。\mathbb Z[\sqrt{-3}] 不是 UFD。

引理：PID 中主理想升链稳定

若 R 是 PID，且

(a_1)\subseteq(a_2)\subseteq\cdots

是一条主理想升链，则存在 n，使得对所有 i\ge n，

(a_i)=(a_n).

证明。 令

A= igcup_i (a_i).

则 A 是理想。因 R 是 PID，存在 a\in R 使 A=(a)。于是 a\in(a_n) 对某个 n 成立，从而

A=(a)\subseteq(a_n)\subseteq A.

故 (a_i)=(a_n) 对 i\ge n 成立。

定理：PID 是 UFD

若 R 是 PID，则 R 是 UFD。

证明思路。

  1. 存在性。 若 x 不可约则结束。若 x=ab 且 a,b 都非单位，则继续分解。若该过程不停止，会得到严格升链

    (x)\subsetneq(a_1)\subsetneq(a_2)\subsetneq\cdots,

    与升链稳定引理矛盾。

  2. 唯一性。 在 PID 中不可约元为素元。若

    p_1\cdots p_m=q_1\cdots q_n

    是两种不可约分解，则 p_1 整除某个 q_j。因 q_j 不可约，得 p_1\sim q_j。约去后归纳。

5. Euclidean Domain 与最大公因子

定义：Euclidean Domain

整环 R 称为欧几里得整环，若存在函数

N:R\setminus\{0\} o\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}

使得对任意 a,b\in R，b e 0，存在 q,r\in R 满足

a=qb+r,\qquad r=0 ext{ 或 }N(r)<N(b).

其中 q,r 分别称为商和余数。

例

  1. \mathbb Z 是欧几里得整环，取 N(n)=|n|。

  2. Gaussian integers

    \mathbb Z[i]=\{a+bi:a,b\in\mathbb Z\}

    是欧几里得整环，可取

    N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2.

定理：欧几里得整环是 PID

若 R 是欧几里得整环，则 R 是 PID。

证明。

令 I\subseteq R 为非零理想。取 a\in I，使得 N(a) 在 I\setminus{0} 中最小。任取 b\in I，由带余除法

b=qa+r,\qquad r=0 ext{ 或 }N(r)<N(a).

因为 r=b-qa\in I，由 N(a) 的极小性得 r=0。故 b\in(a)，所以 I=(a)。

最大公因子

设 R 为交换环，X\subseteq R。若 d\in R 满足：

  1. d\mid x 对所有 x\in X 成立；
  2. 若 c\mid x 对所有 x\in X 成立，则 c\mid d，

则称 d 为 X 的一个最大公因子，记作 \gcd(X)。在一般环中最大公因子不一定存在。

若 R 是 PID，则任意有限集合 {a_1,\dots,a_n} 的最大公因子存在，并且可写成线性组合：

d=r_1a_1+\cdots+r_na_n.

若 R 是 UFD，则最大公因子也存在：把各 a_i 分解成不可约元的乘积，取各不可约元指数的最小值即可。

6. 分式域

分式域的构造

设 R 是整环。考虑集合

R imes(R\setminus\{0\}).

定义等价关系

(r,s)\sim(r',s')\iffrs'=r's.

记 (r,s) 的等价类为

rac r s.

所有等价类构成 R 的分式域，记作 \operatorname{Frac}(R)。

加法与乘法定义为

rac a b+ rac c d= rac{ad+bc}{bd},\qquad rac a b\cdot rac c d= rac{ac}{bd}.

定理

若 R 是整环，则 \operatorname{Frac}(R) 是域，并且 R 可自然嵌入其中：

R\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Frac}(R),\qquadr\mapsto rac r1.

例

\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z)=\mathbb Q,\qquad\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[i])=\mathbb Q(i).

7. 多项式环与形式幂级数

多项式环

设 R 为环，

R[x]=\left\{\sum_{i=0}^n a_ix^i:a_i\in R,\ n\ge 0 ight\}.

若

f=\sum_{i=0}^n a_ix^i,\qquad a_n e 0,

则 a_n 称为首项系数，a_0 称为常数项，n 称为次数，记作 \deg f=n。

若 R 为交换环，则 R[x] 也是交换环。

多元多项式环可迭代定义：

R[x_1,\dots,x_n]\cong R[x_1,\dots,x_{n-1}][x_n].

代入同态

设 \varphi:R o S 是环同态，且 s_1,\dots,s_n\in S 与 \varphi(R) 中元素可交换，则存在唯一环同态

\overline\varphi:R[x_1,\dots,x_n] o S

满足

\overline\varphi|_R=\varphi,\qquad\overline\varphi(x_i)=s_i.

形式幂级数

形式幂级数环定义为

Rx=\left\{\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}a_ix^i:a_i\in R ight\}.

命题：

  1. 若

    f=\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}a_ix^i\in Rx,

    则

    f\in Rx^ imes\iffa_0\in R^ imes.

  2. 若 a_0 在 R 中不可约，则 f 在 Rx 中不可约。

注意：a_0 不是单位并不必然推出 f 不可约；例如 1+x 在 \mathbb Zx 中是单位。

8. 多项式环中的因子分解

单项式与次数

在多元多项式环 R[x_1,\dots,x_n] 中，单项式形如

x_1^{k_1}\cdots x_n^{k_n}.

总次数定义为

k_1+\cdots+k_n.

零多项式次数约定为 -\infty；非零常数次数为 0。

若 R 是整环，则 R[x_1,\dots,x_n] 也是整环，并且

\deg(fg)=\deg f+\deg g.

一元多项式的带余除法

设 R 为交换环，g\in R[x] 的首项系数为单位。则对任意 f\in R[x]，存在唯一 q,r\in R[x]，使得

f=qg+r,\qquad \deg r<\deg g.

特别地，若 F 是域，则 F[x] 是欧几里得整环，因此是 PID。

推论

若 F 是域，则

F[x]^ imes=F^ imes.

即 F[x] 的单位正是非零常数多项式。

但 F[x,y] 不是 PID，例如 (x,y) 不是主理想。因此 F[x,y] 不是欧几里得整环。不过

F[x_1,\dots,x_n]

仍然是 UFD。

根与因式定理

设 f\in R[x]，c\in R。若 f(c)=0，则称 c 为 f 在 R 中的根。

若 R 为交换环，则

f(c)=0\iffx-c\mid f(x).

根的个数

若 D 是整环，f\in D[x] 且 \deg f=n，则 f 在 D 中至多有 n 个根。

证明思路。 对次数归纳。若 c 是根，则 f=(x-c)g，且 \deg g=n-1。若 c’ e c 也是根，则

0=f(c')=(c'-c)g(c').

因 D 是整环且 c’-c e 0，得 g(c’)=0。

多重根与导数

设 D 为整环，f\in D[x]。若

f(x)=(x-c)^m g(x),\qquad g(c) e 0,

则称 m 为根 c 的重数。m=1 时称单根，m>1 时称重根。

形式导数定义为

\left(\sum_{i=0}^n a_ix^i ight)'=\sum_{i=1}^n ia_ix^{i-1}.

若 D 是整环，则

c ext{ 是 } f ext{ 的重根}\ifff(c)=0 ext{ 且 } f'(c)=0.

若 D 是域，则 f 无重根等价于 f 与 f’ 互素：

\gcd(f,f')=1.

例：在 \mathbb Q[x] 中，f(x)=x^2+1 的导数为 2x，故无重根。

有理根判别

设 D 是 UFD，F=\operatorname{Frac}(D)。若

f(x)=a_nx^n+\cdots+a_0\in D[x]

且 c/d\in F 是 f 的根，其中 \gcd(c,d)=1，则

c\mid a_0,\qquad d\mid a_n.

例

对

f(x)=x^4+3x^3-6x^2-11x-6\in\mathbb Z[x],

有理根只能从

\pm1,\ \pm2,\ \pm3,\ \pm6

中寻找。

9. Gauss 引理与 Eisenstein 判别法

内容与本原多项式

设 D 是 UFD，

f(x)=\sum_{i=0}^n a_ix^i\in D[x].

系数 a_0,\dots,a_n 的最大公因子称为 f 的内容，记作 c(f)。若 c(f) 是单位，则称 f 为本原多项式。

Gauss 引理

若 D 是 UFD，f,g\in D[x]，则

c(fg)\sim c(f)c(g).

特别地，本原多项式的乘积仍是本原多项式。

Gauss 引理的推论

设 D 是 UFD，F=\operatorname{Frac}(D)。若 f,g\in D[x] 均为本原多项式，则

f\sim g ext{ in }D[x]\ifff\sim g ext{ in }F[x].

若 f\in D[x] 为本原多项式，则

f ext{ 在 }D[x] ext{ 中不可约}\ifff ext{ 在 }F[x] ext{ 中不可约}.

定理：D[x] 是 UFD

若 D 是 UFD，则 D[x] 也是 UFD。

因此反复归纳可得：

D[x_1,\dots,x_n]

也是 UFD。

Eisenstein 判别法

设 D 是 UFD，F=\operatorname{Frac}(D)，

f(x)=a_nx^n+\cdots+a_0\in D[x],\qquad \deg f\ge 1.

若存在素元 p\in D 满足：

  1. p mid a_n；
  2. p\mid a_i 对所有 0\le i\le n-1 成立；
  3. p^2 mid a_0，

则 f 在 F[x] 中不可约。若 f 本原，则 f 在 D[x] 中也不可约。

例

令

f(x)=2x^3-6x^2+9x-15\in\mathbb Z[x].

取 p=3，有 3 mid 2，3\mid -6,9,-15，且 9 mid -15。故 f 在 \mathbb Q[x] 中不可约。

10. 群作用

定义：群作用

设 G 为群，S 为集合。G 在 S 上的一个左作用是映射

G imes S o S,\qquad (g,x)\mapsto gx

满足：

  1. ex=x；
  2. g_1(g_2x)=(g_1g_2)x。

记作

G\curvearrowright S.

例

  1. S_n 作用在 {1,2,\dots,n} 上：

    (\sigma,i)\mapsto \sigma(i).

  2. G 作用在自身上：

    • 左平移：(h,g)\mapsto hg；
    • 右平移：(h,g)\mapsto gh^{-1}；
    • 共轭作用：(h,g)\mapsto hgh^{-1}。

  3. 若 H\le G，则 H 作用在 G 上：

    (h,g)\mapsto hg

    其轨道为左陪集。

  4. 若 H\le G，H 作用在 G 上：

    (h,g)\mapsto gh^{-1}

    可看作右平移。

  5. 若 H\le G，H 作用在子群集合上：

    (h,K)\mapsto hKh^{-1},

    称为共轭作用。

轨道与稳定子

设 G\curvearrowright S。定义关系

x\sim y\iff\exists g\in G,\ gx=y.

这是等价关系。

元素 x 的轨道为

Gx=\{gx:g\in G\}.

稳定子为

G_x=\{g\in G:gx=x\}.

稳定子 G_x 是 G 的子群。

轨道-稳定子定理

若 G\curvearrowright S，则

|Gx|=[G:G_x].

证明。 映射

G/G_x o Gx,\qquad gG_x\mapsto gx

良定义且为双射。

共轭类、中心化子、正规化子

若 G 作用在自身上：

h\cdot g=hgh^{-1},

则 g 的轨道称为 g 的共轭类：

\operatorname{Cl}(g)=\{hgh^{-1}:h\in G\}.

稳定子称为 g 的中心化子：

C_G(g)=\{h\in G:hg=gh\}.

若 K\le G，共轭作用在子群集合上：

h\cdot K=hKh^{-1}.

其稳定子为 K 的正规化子：

N_G(K)=\{g\in G:gKg^{-1}=K\}.

有

K riangleleft N_G(K)\le G.

类方程

设 G 为有限群，x_1,\dots,x_r 为所有非中心元素的共轭类代表，则

|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum_{i=1}^r [G:C_G(x_i)].

若 K\le G，则 K 的共轭子群个数为

[G:N_G(K)].

群作用诱导置换表示

若 G\curvearrowright S，则得到群同态

heta:G o A(S),

其中 A(S) 是 S 上所有双射构成的群，且

heta(g)(s)=gs.

Cayley 定理

任意群 G 都同构于某个置换群。若 |G|=n，则

G\hookrightarrow S_n.

证明。 取 G 对自身的左平移作用，得到同态

G o A(G).

若 g_1 e g_2，则 heta(g_1)(e)=g_1 e g_2= heta(g_2)(e)，故该同态单射。

自同构与内自同构

记

\operatorname{Aut}(G)=\{f:G o G:f ext{ 是同构}\}.

共轭作用给出同态

G o \operatorname{Aut}(G),\qquadg\mapsto igl(h\mapsto ghg^{-1} igr).

其核为中心：

\ker=Z(G).

因此

G/Z(G)\cong \operatorname{Inn}(G)\le \operatorname{Aut}(G).

11. Sylow 定理

引理：有限 p-群作用的固定点

设 H 为 p-群，H\curvearrowright S，且 S 有限。令

S_0=\{x\in S:hx=x ext{ for all }h\in H\}.

则

|S_0|\equiv |S|\pmod p.

理由。 S 分解为若干轨道。轨道大小为

|\mathcal O_x|=[H:H_x].

若轨道不是单点，则其大小被 p 整除。

Cauchy 定理

若 G 为有限群，素数 p 满足 p\mid |G|，则存在 a\in G，使得

|a|=p.

证明可考虑集合

S=\{(a_1,\dots,a_p)\in G^p:a_1a_2\cdots a_p=e\},

并令 \mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z 通过循环移位作用在 S 上。固定点给出阶为 p 的元素。

定义：p-群与 Sylow p-子群

若有限群 G 满足

|G|=p^n

则称 G 为 p-群。

若 |G|=p^n m，其中 (p,m)=1，则 G 的阶为 p^n 的子群称为 Sylow p-子群。

p-群的中心

若 G 是有限 p-群，则

|Z(G)|>1.

证明。 令 G 对自身作共轭作用。类方程为

|G|=|Z(G)|+\sum_i |\operatorname{Cl}(x_i)|.

非中心元素的共轭类大小都被 p 整除，且 p\mid |G|，故 p\mid |Z(G)|。由于 e\in Z(G)，得 |Z(G)|>1。

正规化子引理

若 G 为有限群，H<G 为 p-子群，则

N_G(H) e H.

更精确地，在合适假设下

[N_G(H):H]\equiv [G:H]\pmod p.

因此若 p\mid [G:H]，则 N_G(H) 严格大于 H。

第一 Sylow 定理

设

|G|=p^n m,\qquad (p,m)=1.

则 G 含有阶为 p^i 的子群，对所有 0\le i\le n 成立。特别地，G 含有 Sylow p-子群。

并且可构造链

H_1 riangleleft H_2 riangleleft\cdots riangleleft H_n,\qquad |H_i|=p^i.

第二 Sylow 定理

设 |G|=p^n m，(p,m)=1。

  1. 任意 Sylow p-子群的共轭仍是 Sylow p-子群；
  2. 任意两个 Sylow p-子群彼此共轭；
  3. 若 Sylow p-子群唯一，则它在 G 中正规。

第三 Sylow 定理

设 n_p 为 G 的 Sylow p-子群个数。若

|G|=p^n m,\qquad (p,m)=1,

则

n_p\mid m,\qquad n_p\equiv 1\pmod p.

例：阶为 28 的群

若 |G|=28=2^2\cdot 7，则 Sylow 7-子群个数 n_7 满足

n_7\mid 4,\qquad n_7\equiv 1\pmod 7.

故 n_7=1，所以 G 含有正规 Sylow 7-子群。

例：S_4 的 Sylow 3-子群

因为

|S_4|=24=2^3\cdot 3,

Sylow 3-子群个数 n_3 满足

n_3\mid 8,\qquad n_3\equiv 1\pmod 3.

故 n_3=1 或 4。事实上 S_4 中的 Sylow 3-子群由 3-循环生成，共有四个：

\langle(123) angle,\quad\langle(124) angle,\quad\langle(134) angle,\quad\langle(234) angle.

12. 模

定义：左模

设 R 为环，A 为交换群。若有映射

R imes A o A,\qquad (r,a)\mapsto ra

满足：

  1. r(a+b)=ra+rb；
  2. (r+s)a=ra+sa；
  3. (rs)a=r(sa)；
  4. 1a=a，

则称 A 为一个左 R-模。

例

  1. 当 R=\mathbb Z 时，\mathbb Z-模恰好是交换群。
  2. 若 R=k 是域，则 k-模就是 k 上的向量空间。

  3. 若 f:R o S 是环同态，则 S 可看作 R-模，标量作用为

    r\cdot s=f(r)s.

  4. 左理想正是 R 的左 R-子模。

零模

对任意 a\in A 和 r\in R，

0_Ra=0_A,\qquad r0_A=0_A.

通常记零子模为 0 或 {0}。

模同态

设 A,B 为 R-模。映射 f:A o B 称为 R-模同态，若

  1. f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)；
  2. f(ra)=rf(a)。

即

f(rx+sy)=rf(x)+sf(y).

单同态、满同态、同构按通常方式定义。

核与像

若 f:A o B 是 R-模同态，则

\ker f=\{a\in A:f(a)=0\}

是 A 的子模，

\operatorname{Im}f=f(A)

是 B 的子模。

生成子模

若 X\subseteq A，则由 X 生成的子模记作

\langle X angle.

若 X={a_1,\dots,a_n}，则

\langle a_1,\dots,a_n angle=\left\{\sum_{i=1}^n r_ia_i:r_i\in R ight\}.

特别地，

\langle a angle=Ra=\{ra:r\in R\}.

商模与第一同构定理

若 N\subseteq M 是子 R-模，则

M/N

在

r(m+N)=rm+N

下成为 R-模，称为商模。

若 f:A o B 是 R-模同态，则

A/\ker f\cong \operatorname{Im}f.

第二、第三同构定理

若 B,C\subseteq A 是子模，则

B/(B\cap C)\cong (B+C)/C.

若 B\subseteq C\subseteq A，则

(A/B)/(C/B)\cong A/C.

正合列

模同态列

A\xrightarrow{f}B\xrightarrow{g}C

称在 B 处正合，若

\operatorname{Im}f=\ker g.

短正合列是形如

0\longrightarrow A\xrightarrow{f}B\xrightarrow{g}C\longrightarrow 0

的正合列。此时 f 单射，g 满射，并且

B/\operatorname{Im}f\cong C.

短五引理

考虑交换图

egin{array}{ccccccccc}0&\longrightarrow&A&\xrightarrow{f}&B&\xrightarrow{g}&C&\longrightarrow&0\&&\downarrow\alpha&&\downarrow eta&&\downarrow\gamma&&\0&\longrightarrow&A'&\xrightarrow{f'}&B'&\xrightarrow{g'}&C'&\longrightarrow&0\end{array}

若两行短正合，则：

  1. 若 \alpha,\gamma 单射，则 eta 单射；
  2. 若 \alpha,\gamma 满射，则 eta 满射；
  3. 若 \alpha,\gamma 是同构，则 eta 是同构。

证明方法是 diagram chasing。

13. 域扩张

动机

二次方程

ax^2+bx+c=0

有通用求根公式；三次、四次方程也有根式公式。但五次及以上一般没有根式公式。这推动我们研究域扩张与多项式方程的根。

定义：域扩张

若 K\subseteq F 且 K,F 都是域，并且 K 的运算与 F 中继承的运算一致，则称 F 是 K 的一个域扩张，记作

F/K.

此时 F 自然是 K 上的向量空间。其维数称为扩张次数，记作

[F:K]=\dim_K F.

若 [F:K]<\infty，称 F/K 为有限扩张。

例

  1. \mathbb C/\mathbb R 是次数为 2 的扩张：

    [\mathbb C:\mathbb R]=2.

  2. \mathbb R/\mathbb Q 不是有限扩张：

    [\mathbb R:\mathbb Q]=\infty.

备注

域扩张中的“次数”类似群论中子群指数的概念，但要注意：选取不同基底只改变表示方式，不改变维数。

entry.title: Le carnet de quartier
entry.content:
Niveau

A1-A2. Texte original court pour lecture quotidienne.

Texte

Chaque vendredi, Lina marche dans son quartier avec un petit carnet bleu. Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle. Ce matin, elle sort après le petit-déjeuner et ferme doucement la porte. Dans la rue des Pins, une boulangère met des croissants dans la vitrine. Lina écrit: “La boulangerie ouvre à sept heures.” Plus loin, deux enfants jouent devant l’école, et un homme arrose les fleurs de son balcon. Lina écoute les sons: un vélo, un bus, puis le rire d’une voisine. Elle dessine aussi un banc sous un grand arbre, parce qu’elle aime lire à cet endroit. À midi, elle achète une soupe simple au café du coin. Le serveur lui demande si elle habite ici depuis longtemps. Lina répond: “Non, seulement depuis un mois, mais je me sens déjà bien.” L’après-midi, elle rentre chez elle et relit ses notes. Elle ajoute trois mots nouveaux: calme, vitrine et voisinage. Le soir, elle téléphone à sa soeur et décrit son quartier en français. Grâce à ce carnet, Lina remarque les petits détails et parle avec plus de confiance.

Traduction phrase par phrase

Français中文
Chaque vendredi, Lina marche dans son quartier avec un petit carnet bleu.每个星期五，Lina 带着一本蓝色小笔记本在她的街区散步。
Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle.她想更好地了解家附近的街道。
Ce matin, elle sort après le petit-déjeuner et ferme doucement la porte.今天早上，她吃完早餐后出门，并轻轻关上门。
Dans la rue des Pins, une boulangère met des croissants dans la vitrine.在松树街，一位女面包师把羊角面包放进橱窗。
Lina écrit: “La boulangerie ouvre à sept heures.”Lina 写道：“面包店七点开门。”
Plus loin, deux enfants jouent devant l’école, et un homme arrose les fleurs de son balcon.再往前，两个孩子在学校前玩耍，一个男人给阳台上的花浇水。
Lina écoute les sons: un vélo, un bus, puis le rire d’une voisine.Lina 听着这些声音：一辆自行车、一辆公交车，然后是一位女邻居的笑声。
Elle dessine aussi un banc sous un grand arbre, parce qu’elle aime lire à cet endroit.她还画了一张大树下的长椅，因为她喜欢在这个地方读书。
À midi, elle achète une soupe simple au café du coin.中午，她在街角咖啡馆买了一份简单的汤。
Le serveur lui demande si elle habite ici depuis longtemps.服务员问她是否已经在这里住了很久。
Lina répond: “Non, seulement depuis un mois, mais je me sens déjà bien.”Lina 回答：“没有，只住了一个月，但我已经感觉很舒服了。”
L’après-midi, elle rentre chez elle et relit ses notes.下午，她回到家，重新读自己的笔记。
Elle ajoute trois mots nouveaux: calme, vitrine et voisinage.她添加了三个新词：安静、橱窗和邻里。
Le soir, elle téléphone à sa soeur et décrit son quartier en français.晚上，她给姐姐打电话，并用法语描述自己的街区。
Grâce à ce carnet, Lina remarque les petits détails et parle avec plus de confiance.多亏了这本笔记本，Lina 注意到小细节，说话也更有信心。

Vocabulaire

Mot / Expression中文Note
quartier街区；社区dans mon quartier = 在我的街区
carnet笔记本un petit carnet bleu
près de在……附近près de chez elle = 在她家附近
vitrine橱窗la vitrine d’un magasin
arroser浇水arroser les fleurs
banc长椅un banc sous un arbre
café du coin街角咖啡馆du coin = 附近的、本地的
depuis自从；已经depuis un mois = 已经一个月
calme安静的；平静une rue calme
confiance信心parler avec confiance

Grammaire

PointExplication
Chaque + nom singulier表示“每一个”，后面接单数名词：chaque vendredi。
près de chez + pronom表示“在某人家附近”：près de chez elle。
depuis + durée表示一个状态持续了多久：depuis un mois。
grâce à + nom表示“多亏；由于”：grâce à ce carnet。

Phrases utiles

Phrase中文
Elle veut mieux connaître les rues près de chez elle.她想更好地了解家附近的街道。
Je me sens déjà bien.我已经感觉很舒服了。
Elle décrit son quartier en français.她用法语描述自己的街区。
Elle parle avec plus de confiance.她说话更有信心。

entry.title: Gilbert White on Swallows and Observation
entry.content:
Source

Gilbert White, The Natural History and Antiquities of Selborne (1789), public domain. Passage adapted from White’s letters on swallows, migration, and local observation.

Passage

In Gilbert White’s account of Selborne, the arrival of swallows is not a decorative sign of spring but a problem that requires careful evidence. The birds appear suddenly in April, skim over meadows and ponds through the warm months, and then vanish when autumn advances. A careless observer might explain this pattern with a simple rule: the birds come when the weather is mild and leave when it grows cold. White treats that rule as only a starting point. He wants to know which species arrive first, where they feed, how they build, and whether every bird truly departs.

His method depends on repeated attention to a familiar place. Instead of collecting a single dramatic incident, he compares many small observations: the height at which swallows fly before rain, the insects they follow, the dates on which nests are occupied, and the behavior of young birds near the end of summer. Such details matter because a broad theory of migration can be tested only by facts that seem ordinary at first. If one swallow is seen late in the year, the sighting may be an accident; if several are recorded in the same conditions, the pattern becomes harder to ignore.

White also shows why scientific caution is valuable. In his time, some people believed that swallows slept through winter hidden in banks, hollow trees, or even beneath water. Others argued that the birds migrated to warmer regions. White does not dismiss either claim merely because it sounds strange or familiar. He asks what each explanation would predict and what local evidence can confirm. The question is not settled by imagination, but by the patient comparison of season, weather, species, and behavior.

The result is a model of natural history as disciplined noticing. Selborne is small, yet it becomes a field laboratory because the observer returns to the same lanes, roofs, streams, and hedges year after year. The value of the place lies in continuity. When observations accumulate, they reveal changes that no single walk could prove: the timing of arrivals, the relation between insects and flight, and the difference between rumor and evidence. White’s swallows therefore teach a larger lesson. Knowledge often begins when a common event is treated not as background scenery, but as a question that deserves exact description.

Vocabulary

Word / PhraseMeaningTOEFL Note
decorative signsomething noticed mainly for beauty or symbolismThe phrase is contrasted with evidence-based inquiry.
starting pointan initial idea, not a final answerUseful when an author refines a simple explanation.
repeated attentioncareful observation over timeA key method in natural history and field science.
dramatic incidenta striking single eventOften contrasted with accumulated data.
predictsay what should happen if an explanation is correctImportant in passages about testing hypotheses.
continuityregular connection over timeExplains why repeated observation has scientific value.

Sentence Work

Such details matter because a broad theory of migration can be tested only by facts that seem ordinary at first.

The main structure is cause and condition: details matter because.... The sentence explains why small observations are not trivial. The phrase can be tested only by shows necessity; without ordinary facts, the larger theory remains unproved.

White does not dismiss either claim merely because it sounds strange or familiar.

This sentence highlights scientific neutrality. The pattern not... merely because... warns readers that an idea should not be accepted or rejected by appearance alone. Evidence, not habit or surprise, controls the argument.

Structure Notes

The passage begins with a familiar seasonal event and turns it into a research question. It then explains White’s method of repeated local observation, contrasts single incidents with accumulated evidence, and places the debate over swallows within a larger lesson about cautious scientific reasoning. The final paragraph broadens the example from one village to the general value of disciplined noticing.

Writing Takeaway

When writing about observation, choose a common phenomenon, show why a simple explanation is incomplete, list the kinds of evidence needed, and end by stating the larger habit of thought the example teaches.
